claims.text: The author ran a 40-person engineering organization through both an in-office regime and a fully remote regime.
Remote work is bad for junior developers.
claims.stance_source: the common claim the author keeps encountering (unnamed critics of remote work)

claims.text: The author's junior developer cohort had a time-to-first-production-commit of 24 days during the in-office era.
The author's junior developer cohort had a time-to-first-production-commit of 26 days after going remote.

The difference between the junior cohort's 24-day in-office time-to-first-production-commit and its 26-day remote time-to-first-production-commit is statistically indistinguishable given the small sample.
Retention of the author's junior developers at the two-year mark rose after going remote, from 61% to 78%.
The rise in the author's junior developer two-year retention under remote work was mostly because the organization could hire people who live where housing is affordable.
Junior developers learn by overhearing senior conversations in the office.
claims.stance_source: the standard 'osmosis' counterargument to remote work

claims.text: Nobody who makes the osmosis argument that juniors learn by overhearing senior conversations ever measures that effect.
Learning by osmosis in the office most likely benefited mainly the loudest office cliques.
The author's organization introduced mandatory written design docs for every feature after going remote.
The author's organization introduced a rule that all debugging sessions happen on a recorded call anyone can join.
Mandatory written design docs and recorded, open debugging calls more than replaced the hallway learning the author's organization lost by going remote.
A junior developer who watches ten recorded debugging sessions learns more than a junior who eavesdrops on senior conversations for a month.
Fully remote work is probably worse for the bottom decile of self-starters, the junior developers who need someone to notice when they are stuck.
If an organization's hiring cannot screen for autonomy, then hybrid work with two anchor days is likely the safer default.
The blanket claim that remote work kills junior development is a management failure dressed up as a law of nature.

theses.text: The blanket claim that remote work kills junior development is false; poor junior outcomes under remote work reflect a management failure, not an inherent law of nature.

The author's own data from running a 40-person engineering organization through both regimes shows that going fully remote did not harm junior developers: their time-to-first-production-commit was statistically unchanged and their two-year retention actually rose.

With deliberate structure such as mandatory written design docs and recorded, openly joinable debugging calls, a remote organization can more than replace the informal 'osmosis' learning juniors lose from the office.
Fully remote work is worse mainly for the least self-directed juniors, so an organization whose hiring cannot screen for autonomy should default to hybrid work with two anchor days.
